one_line_takeaway: PANDORA shows that poisoning a custom GPT's RAG knowledge base plus a retrieval-forcing system prompt jailbreaks OpenAI GPTs far more effectively (64.3% GPT-3.5 / 34.8% GPT-4) than direct prompting (3.0% / 1.0%), exposing that safety filters cover text inputs but not RAG-retrieved content.
key_finding: PANDORA against GPTs achieves an average jailbreak success rate of 64.3% on the GPT-3.5 backend and 34.8% on the GPT-4 backend, versus only 3.0% (GPT-3.5) and 1.0% (GPT-4) for naive/direct malicious questions replayed on ChatGPT with the same models — roughly a 20x and 35x improvement. Privacy Violation was consistently the easiest category (35.3% average across the four groups), and GPT-4-backed targets were substantially harder than GPT-3.5, attributed to GPT-4's improved alignment.
core_contribution: The paper introduces RAG Poisoning, a novel indirect jailbreak vector against LLM-integrated apps that use Retrieval-Augmented Generation, and a proof-of-concept tool named PANDORA that operationalizes it against OpenAI's custom GPTs. Rather than attacking the model with an adversarial input prompt (the direct jailbreak line of prior work such as GCG [Zou], MasterKey [Deng], AutoDAN [Liu], which the paper cites as related work rather than claims as its own), PANDORA poisons the external knowledge base the GPT retrieves from. The attacker builds a custom GPT, uploads maliciously crafted documents as its knowledge source, and installs an in-built system prompt that forces every user query to be answered by retrieving and paraphrasing the tainted documents. This turns a benign RAG feature into a delivery channel for policy-violating content that bypasses the text-input safety filters, which the paper observes are not applied to RAG-retrieved content.
threat_model: The adversary is the CREATOR of a custom GPT (a GPT Builder), not merely an end user. They can (1) generate policy-violating source material, (2) upload arbitrary files as the GPT's knowledge base, and (3) author the GPT's system/in-built prompt. The victim surface is OpenAI's GPTs platform and its content-moderation filters; the goal is to produce a shareable GPT instance that, for any end-user question in a prohibited category, emits harmful content while evading OpenAI's input/output moderation. The attack is black-box (no model weights or gradients). Key exploited gap: safety filters are enforced on direct text prompts but not on content ingested via RAG, and GPTs process one uploaded file at a time selected largely by filename, which the attacker games.
limitations: The authors themselves label this a work-in-progress / preliminary paper. (1) Scale is very small and forced by platform rate limits (GPTs capped at 40 queries per 3 hours), so only 100 tests per scenario. (2) The knowledge base is crafted manually — the authors list automation as future work. (3) The method is black-box with no interpretability of WHY poisoning succeeds; a white-box analysis is deferred. (4) Success is scored by manual human inspection against two subjective criteria (Relevance, Content Quality), with no inter-rater reliability reported and no automated/standardized judge. (5) No standardized public benchmark is used; the four categories and prompts are self-constructed following MasterKey [Deng et al.]. (6) No mitigation is proposed or evaluated (only named as future work), and results are on the GPTs platform snapshot at submission time (Feb 2024), so durability against updated OpenAI moderation is unaddressed.

datasets_used: Self-constructed prompt set: 10 unique jailbreak prompts per category, run 10 iterations each = 100 tests per scenario, over 5 rounds
Four OpenAI policy-violation categories adopted from MasterKey [Deng et al.]: Adult Content, Harmful and Abusive Content, Privacy Violation, Illegal Content
Web-crawled top-ranked search-engine content for policy-violating keywords (attack material source, not an evaluation benchmark)
Mistral-7B used as an uncensored LLM to generate targeted malicious content
quantitative_results: PANDORA on GPTs / GPT-3.5 average success: 64.3% (Adult 58.0%, Harmful 62.0%, Privacy 78.0%, Illegal 59.0%)
PANDORA on GPTs / GPT-4 average success: 34.8% (Adult 19.0%, Harmful 23.0%, Privacy 56.0%, Illegal 41.0%)
Direct baseline on GPT-3.5 average: 3.0% (Adult 1.0%, Harmful 2.0%, Privacy 6.0%, Illegal 3.0%)
Direct baseline on GPT-4 average: 1.0% (Adult 0.0%, Harmful 0.0%, Privacy 1.0%, Illegal 3.0%)
Privacy category is the easiest to jailbreak overall: 35.3% average across the four comparison groups
Overall grand average across all patterns/scenarios in Table I: 25.7%
baselines_compared: Direct / naive malicious-question jailbreak replayed on ChatGPT powered by GPT-3.5 (3.0% avg)
Direct / naive malicious-question jailbreak replayed on ChatGPT powered by GPT-4-turbo (1.0% avg)
